video_title: Why Hyperliquid Is the "Gold Standard" | REKT Vision with @blocmates
gist: Grant of Blocmates explains his early (2023) Hyperliquid thesis: a purpose-built, product-market-fit DEX with a near-fair launch that has become arguably the most profitable business per employee on the planet, with FDV ~$62B closing in on BNB. He extends the 'equal and opposite reaction' framework to the privacy-coin narrative (Zcash/Monero), is sharply critical of Ethereum's lack of direction versus Solana/Tempo, and is bullish on stablecoin/agentic micropayments while warning retail will get wrecked on the coming OpenAI/Anthropic/SpaceX IPOs.

insights.claim: Hyperliquid was identified as an early winner by Blocmates in 2023 specifically because it was a purpose-built, single-use-case chain rather than another general-purpose blockspace L1, which they judged to be hitting saturation.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: The winning bet was on application-specific chains with real product-market fit over generic L1s — a template for screening future early-stage crypto teams.
insights.quote: we felt like the demand for that general purpose block space was kind of hitting like a plateau, a s saturation and we thought well what's the again what's the equal and opposite reaction to that so purpose-built

insights.claim: Grant's framework for picking early-stage crypto winners is that the team must be doing something new, novel and unique rather than replicating a well-trodden playbook, because copycat projects hit a law of diminishing returns.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: A repeatable filter for early-stage crypto allocation: weight novelty and founder conviction over me-too execution.
insights.quote: It has to be new. It has to be novel. It has to be unique and people have to be thinking a little bit outside the box

insights.claim: Hyperliquid is described as a best-in-class product whose go-to-market — galvanizing a community and giving a large share of token supply to the market rather than defending an inflated price — was as important to its success as the technology.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Product quality alone is insufficient; investors should weight token distribution design and community alignment as core to durable value.
insights.quote: obviously the product's killer. It's like it's best-in-class product, but then that also has to match up with how you go to market

insights.claim: Hyperliquid's team was able to self-fund because they ran a very successful market-making business before launching, letting them avoid VC funding and offer a near-fair launch — a precondition Grant notes is hard to replicate without being 'giga-rich' already.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: The fair-launch model is gated by founders' pre-existing wealth, so it will stay rare — scarcity that makes genuine fair launches a premium signal.
insights.quote: I think the Hyperlid team had a very very successful market making business before this

insights.claim: Hyperliquid's FDV was ~$62 billion at the time of recording (HYPE ~$65), with Grant noting only USDC and XRP (about 2x higher) ranked above it and that it is closing in on BNB.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Sets concrete valuation anchors; a 'flippening' of BNB is the next watchable milestone for HYPE holders.
insights.quote: just looking at like FDV it's at what's it 62 billion FDV. I think you've got only USDC higher XRP which is 2x from here. Um yeah it's closing in on BMB

insights.claim: Grant expects HYPE catching BNB to become a self-fulfilling prophecy and warns that take-profit selling is likely as the two market caps/FDVs converge, given Jeff was in an early Binance Accelerator cohort and the rivalry is now head-to-head.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Traders should anticipate profit-taking resistance as HYPE's FDV approaches BNB's, even if the long-term narrative is bullish.
insights.quote: people will probably have take profit targets as those uh market caps and FDVS converge

insights.claim: Grant calls Hyperliquid arguably the most profitable business on the planet on a revenue-per-employee basis, reportedly run by only 12-15 people.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Extreme revenue-per-head is the fundamental underpinning the bull case; a key metric to track versus traditional exchanges.
insights.quote: if not the most profitable business on the planet per revenue per per employee

insights.claim: Hyperliquid has surpassed Coinbase's volume and competes with Binance's daily volume, and the head of the NYSE reportedly called it bigger than the NASDAQ despite running on ~12-15 people.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder); host
insights.so_what: A decentralized exchange now rivals incumbent global venues on volume — a structural threat to centralized exchange revenue.
insights.quote: the the the head of New York Stock Exchange basically saying it's bigger than the NASDAQ and it's running on like 12 to 15 people

insights.claim: Hyperliquid added a third revenue stream via a deal with Coinbase and Circle, on top of its DEX and the perps product (which now lets users trade oil, gold and silver).
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Revenue diversification beyond crypto perps (into commodities and a Coinbase/Circle partnership) broadens the addressable market and de-risks the cash flow.
insights.quote: recently they came they have that deal with Coinbase and Circle which you know just essentially adds a third revenue stream to them

insights.claim: Matt Hougan of Bitwise frames Hyperliquid as a 'super app' targeting the $600 trillion global asset market rather than the $3 trillion crypto economy.
insights.speaker: host (citing Matt Hougan, Bitwise)
insights.so_what: If the TAM reframing holds, current FDV may still be early relative to a $600T target — the core long-term bull case.
insights.quote: Hyperlquid is not a crypto app. It's a super app. It's not targeting the $3 trillion crypto economy. It's targeting the $600 trillion global asset market.

insights.claim: Grant frames HYPE's appeal via an analogy: it is as if Base were an L1 that took 97% of Coinbase trading fees and used them to buy back and burn the token — implying aggressive fee-driven value accrual.
insights.speaker: host / Grant
insights.so_what: The buyback-and-burn fee mechanism is the value-accrual engine; investors should track fee capture and burn rate as the key fundamental.
insights.quote: what if Bass was just an L1 and they said... we're going to take 97% of our fees and buy back and burn base. Like, how bullish would people be on that token? Like, that's literally hype.

insights.claim: Grant argues markets are mispricing high-revenue crypto assets, noting that on fundamentals Hyperliquid should rank top-five or even top-three, while low-revenue tokens persist 'for no apparent reason.'
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Implies further re-rating upside for revenue-generating tokens versus zombie tokens as the market matures toward fundamentals.
insights.quote: on paper you would expect to see it in top five, top maybe even three. But, you know, thinking too logically sometimes can get you killed

insights.claim: Grant argues the VC low-float/high-FDV token model broke retail trust: tokens launched at 3-10% float after private price discovery, leaving buyers defending unrealistic valuations that consistently bled lower ('VC coins').
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Low-float/high-FDV launches are structurally bearish for late buyers; screen new listings for float and FDV before participating.
insights.quote: you'd get a token come to market with, I don't know, three to maybe 10% float supply if you were lucky. Um, price discovery had already happened.

insights.claim: Grant frames the market reaction to VC coins as two opposite extremes: pump.fun-style instant $2 token launches (too far the other way) versus the more pragmatic Hyperliquid path of building a genuinely demanded product with fair, non-gatekept access.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Positions fair-launch product-led tokens as the durable middle path between extractive VC launches and worthless memecoins.
insights.quote: One of them was pumped up fun [pump.fun]... And the and the more ethical and more like pragmatic response to it was something like hyperlquid.

insights.claim: Grant highlights Megaeth's KPI-based vesting — where the team only vests on hitting tangible milestones — as a step in the right direction for aligning founders with token holders.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Milestone-gated vesting is an emerging structure worth watching as a sign of founder alignment in new launches.
insights.quote: I think what Mega Reef [Megaeth] did was pretty interesting where it was like KPI based vesting. So, nobody's going to eat unless we hit like actual tangible milestones.

insights.claim: Grant argues privacy is the next major crypto narrative — potentially bigger than anything before it — because the 'institutions are coming' Bitcoin thesis is now played out and privacy is the last asset class people can buy into on ideological grounds.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Positions privacy coins as the next thematic rotation for ideologically-driven capital; an early-cycle narrative to accumulate.
insights.quote: the next battle which I think is going to be much larger than anything that's came before it is privacy

insights.claim: Grant says the privacy narrative has been building since late Q3 2025 and is a sustained, multi-catalyst theme rather than a flash-in-the-pan, driven by ZK, FHE and TEE tech maturing alongside rising geopolitical tension.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: A durable narrative with converging tech and macro tailwinds is more investable than a single-catalyst pump; tracks back to late Q3 2025 as the start.
insights.quote: this has kind of been ticking over since Q late Q3 2025 and is continuing to be one of the main stairs

insights.claim: Grant discloses he holds a position in Zcash and would like to see it outperform, citing a major late-2024 (November) upgrade around shielded transactions and a strong mobile-first wallet, while declining to rank privacy coins on tech.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: A named position with a stated catalyst (shielded transactions + mobile wallet) — note the disclosed bias when weighing his view.
insights.quote: from like put my investor head on, I'd probably like to see Zcash do a little bit better, but that's only because I've got a bias because I've got a position in it.

insights.claim: Old-guard Bitcoin cypherpunks including the Winklevoss twins and Balaji are reportedly rotating capital into Zcash, signaling smart-money interest in privacy coins.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Named smart-money flows into Zcash are a leading indicator that retail typically follows later.
insights.quote: We're seeing some of the old Bitcoin like cipher funks... moving from Bitcoin into Zcash... Winkle VI, Bellagi, all of them.

insights.claim: Grant's broader framework is that real wealth is made buying narratives at the lows in bear markets (e.g. Solana at $8-10 last cycle) before they hit CNBC/CNN, not after mainstream coverage.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Reinforces accumulating emerging narratives (like privacy) early rather than chasing headline-driven tops.
insights.quote: thinking about maybe Salana last cycle 8 to10... that's where the true meat is actually made it's not when you start seeing it all on CNN CNBC

insights.claim: Grant is sharply critical of Ethereum, arguing it has lost direction through constant pivots, flip-flopping ideas and leadership changes, and was wrong to be dragged into a throughput battle with Solana it can never win.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: A bearish structural read on ETH relative to faster, more organized chains; weigh against consensus 'ETH near and dear' sentiment.
insights.quote: I think they've been dragged into a throughput conversation with Salana and other highly performing pragmatic chains which which they're never going to win.

insights.claim: Grant warns that if Ethereum keeps selling its treasury at the current rate against a roadmap to 2030, it could run out of funds by 2027 — sending mixed signals to the market.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: A concrete treasury-runway risk (2027) that, if real, undermines ETH's long-horizon roadmap credibility — a fundamental to monitor.
insights.quote: if you keep selling your treasury at the rate you're going to go, you're going to run out of funds by 2027, right?

insights.claim: Grant frames Ethereum's core problem as ideology-versus-pragmatism: ETH is the maximally-decentralized ideological chain, but the market clearly prefers pragmatic, demand-driven chains like Solana and Hyperliquid.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Implies capital rotation toward pragmatic high-throughput chains; the ideology premium is no longer rewarded by price.
insights.quote: you've got this like ideology versus pragmatism battle going on and the market's shown you which one it prefers

insights.claim: Grant flags Tempo — backed by Stripe with $500M behind it, Paradigm involved, and reportedly poaching lead Ethereum researchers — as a serious new competitor with built-in Stripe distribution.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: A well-funded, distribution-rich Stripe-backed chain poaching ETH talent is a competitive threat worth tracking as it launches.
insights.quote: the likes of Tempo which is backed by Stripe and has been porting some of the Ethereum like lead Ethereum researchers... it's got $500 million behind it and paradigm involved

insights.claim: Grant argues Ethereum erred by de-emphasizing DeFi — the financial-primitives use case that originally put it on the map — and should fix its DeFi 'house' first before pursuing artistic and social pursuits.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Suggests the durable value of any smart-contract chain is permissionless financial access; chains that lean into DeFi distribution win.
insights.quote: I also don't like the fact that they've basically done everything they can not to promote what happened in the DeFi sector, which basically put them on the map.

insights.claim: Grant contends Solana's organizational advantage (clear leadership, north star, execution) decisively beats Ethereum, and dismisses the 'Solana is insufficiently decentralized' critique as weak.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Centralized, well-led execution is treated as a feature not a bug — a bull case for SOL versus ETH's diffuse governance.
insights.quote: who's steering the ship, who has the grand vision, what is the north star... If you put them side by side, it's night and day.

insights.claim: Grant calls Near Intents and Near's just-rolled-out confidential transactions two of the most underrated products in crypto, citing strong revenue and excellent UX, and notes Near appears fully vested with no large unlocks pending.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: A multi-tailwind (AI + privacy + no supply overhang) liquid bet; fully-vested supply removes a common overhang risk.
insights.quote: they've got two products that are probably the most two underrated products in the full industry and one of them is Near Intents... the revenue on that thing is absolutely nuts

insights.claim: Grant says Near is capturing multiple tailwinds at once (AI work, intents, confidential transactions, no supply overhang), making it easily justifiable as a liquid open-market purchase.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Stacked tailwinds plus clean tokenomics make NEAR a higher-conviction liquid allocation than single-narrative plays.
insights.quote: any liquid funds very very easily justifiable if you want to go and purchase some near in the open market

insights.claim: Grant predicts subscription pricing dies in favor of headless, pay-per-use micropayments executed by agents pinging APIs and webhooks from chat interfaces (Telegram, WhatsApp, ChatGPT, Claude), naming X402 and Tempo's MPPP as early rails.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Micropayment/agentic-payment rails are an early, explosive opportunity; businesses enabling pay-per-use get first-mover advantage.
insights.quote: the idea of paying for something per month or annually is going to be gone. I think it's going to be pay per use. It's going to be microtransactions.

insights.claim: Grant predicts the next ~100 million on-chain users will be AI agents rather than new humans, making programmable money at the stablecoin level a highly interesting investment theme.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: If agents drive the next user wave, stablecoin and agent-payment infrastructure is the pick-and-shovel play, not human-onboarding apps.
insights.quote: the next 100 million users that are operating particularly on chain are definitely going to be agents as opposed to the new cohort of humans

insights.claim: Grant dismisses the argument that AI agents will spin up their own chains and bypass existing ones, reasoning that human operators control agents and will route them down the path of least resistance using already-built infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Grant / host
insights.so_what: Supports incumbent-chain value accrual from the agentic economy rather than a disruptive new-chain thesis.
insights.quote: it's the human operator that's controlling it... they're going to go the path of least resistance and with infrastructure already built

insights.claim: Grant argues regulation and over-bureaucracy in personalized medicine will, over the next 5-10 years, cause more deaths than it prevents, because AI models can readily map DNA/RNA and design patient-specific antibodies (e.g. CAR-T-style therapy).
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: A bullish, contrarian read on AI-driven personalized medicine and a bet that breakthroughs happen outside heavy regulatory jurisdictions.
insights.quote: I think regulation in the next 5 10 years is going to cause more deaths than it's actually going to save people.

insights.claim: Grant agrees retail will likely get wrecked on the coming AI IPOs, arguing companies like OpenAI, Anthropic and SpaceX stay private longer because they have near-infinite access to private capital (e.g. Stripe-style secondary buyers, an SF house listed for ~$8M in Anthropic exposure instead of cash).
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Implies the public-market entry point for these names will be late-cycle and unfavorable for retail; the gains accrue privately.
insights.quote: once that does run dry, retailer stupid is the is the is the thought.

insights.claim: Grant frames the AI valuation question against Nvidia at ~$5 trillion, arguing there may still be upside but that allocating at trillion-dollar private valuations forces tiny relative returns versus going higher up the stack into consumer, defense tech, robotics, quantum or photonics.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Suggests rotating risk capital toward earlier-stage adjacent narratives (defense, robotics, quantum, photonics) rather than mega-cap AI at peak valuations.
insights.quote: you got to be putting minimill size in at a trillion dollar valuation if you wanted to decent return compared to... going a little bit higher up the stack

insights.claim: Grant warns the AI IPO wave will create a huge liquidity suck as these mega-caps go public, and that Elon Musk's cult-like following lets him take almost anything public — with SpaceX likely going first.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: A large liquidity drain from AI IPOs could pressure other risk assets (including crypto) as capital rotates into the new listings.
insights.quote: I think it might be quite scary when and I a huge liquidity suck when when these things start going live

insights.claim: Grant warns AI usage costs are currently subsidized and risk becoming unaffordable until inference costs fall, with the GPU bottleneck (Nvidia reportedly sold out to 2028) and an electricity/power bottleneck for giga data centers both unresolved.
insights.speaker: host / Grant
insights.so_what: Power and GPU supply are the binding constraints on the agentic economy; energy and compute infrastructure are the durable investment chokepoints.
insights.quote: It's not just Nvidia, you know, priced out till 2028 to get their GPUs into people's hands. It's also where you get the electricity

insights.claim: Grant (and the host) report defaulting away from open-source agent frameworks like Open Claw because OpenAI and Anthropic ship native features (scheduled jobs, mobile/terminal interfaces) overnight, making third-party tooling redundant and not worth the debugging time.
insights.speaker: Grant / host
insights.so_what: Platform incumbents are absorbing open-source agent tooling features, compressing the moat for independent agent wrappers — a competitive risk for that category.
insights.quote: OpenAI and Anthropic and just turn on features overnight... it became kind of like redundant to the point of am I spending more time trying to fix this

insights.claim: Grant sees media as an increasingly strong business vertical in the AI era because distribution, credibility and reputation can't be instantly created by AI models, citing OpenAI's reported acquisition of a media property as evidence incumbents must buy that trust.
insights.speaker: Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)
insights.so_what: Human-led media with distribution and credibility becomes a scarce, acquirable asset as AI commoditizes content — a counter-cyclical bet.
insights.quote: reputation can't just be immediately created by any AI model and they need to be able to purchase that
